s: The tiny island nation of Bahrain contains the world's largest prehistoric cemetery — over 100,000 burial mounds dating back 5,000 years to the ancient Dilmun civilization.
q: Which country has the world's largest prehistoric cemetery?
e: These mounds, some as tall as 15 meters, cover roughly 5% of Bahrain's land area. The Dilmun civilization was a major trading hub between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley, and Bahrain was considered a sacred burial island.

s: The capital of Bangladesh is Dhaka.
q: What is the capital of Bangladesh?
e: Dhaka is the bustling capital of Bangladesh, home to over 171.5 million people!

s: Barbados is located in the Latin America & Caribbean region.
q: In which World Bank region is Barbados located?
e: Barbados, with its capital at Bridgetown, is part of the World Bank's Latin America & Caribbean classification. The country has a population of approximately 282 thousand and is in the Northern and Western Hemispheres.

s: The United States has the most barrier islands in the world, with about 405 along its Atlantic and Gulf coasts.
q: Which country has the most barrier islands in the world?
e: Barrier islands are long, narrow sandy islands that run parallel to the coast. They protect the mainland from storms and waves but are constantly shifting and vulnerable to erosion.

s: Benin is the birthplace of Vodun (Voodoo), a major religion still practiced by about 40% of the population, with an official national Voodoo Day celebrated on January 10.
q: In which country did Vodun (Voodoo) originate?
e: Vodun originated in the Kingdom of Dahomey (present-day Benin) and was carried to the Americas through the slave trade, evolving into Haitian Vodou and Louisiana Voodoo. In Benin, it remains a respected mainstream religion.

s: Bermuda has no natural freshwater rivers or streams — its residents collect rainwater from specially designed white limestone roofs.
q: How do Bermuda residents get their freshwater?
a: By collecting rainwater from white limestone roofs
e: Bermuda law requires all buildings to have stepped white roofs that channel rainwater into underground tanks. Each household relies on this collected water for daily use.

s: Bermuda has no rivers, lakes, or freshwater springs — every home is required by law to have a white stepped roof that funnels rainwater into an underground tank for drinking water.
q: Which Atlantic island territory requires every home to have a white roof for collecting drinking water?
e: Bermuda sits on a coral limestone cap with no natural freshwater sources. The distinctive white limestone roofs are designed to capture rain, which flows down stepped channels into cisterns. Each home must have at least 80% of its roof area for collection.

s: The Bermuda Triangle is a loosely defined region in the western North Atlantic between Miami, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico.
q: Which three points roughly define the Bermuda Triangle?
d: Key West, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico
e: Despite its reputation, the US Coast Guard does not recognize the Bermuda Triangle as a particularly dangerous area. Shipping traffic through the region is heavy and most incidents have conventional explanations.

s: The capital of Bhutan is Thimphu.
q: What is the capital of Bhutan?
e: Thimphu serves as the vibrant capital of Bhutan in Asia!

s: Bhutan is the only country in the world that is carbon negative, absorbing more CO2 than it produces.
q: Which country is the world's only carbon-negative nation?
e: Bhutan's constitution mandates that at least 60% of the country remains forested, and its dense forests absorb far more carbon dioxide than the small nation emits.

s: Bhutan is the only country in the world that is carbon negative, absorbing more CO2 than it produces.
q: Which country is the world's only carbon-negative nation?
e: Bhutan's constitution requires at least 60% of the country to remain forested. Its forests absorb over 6 million tonnes of CO2 annually while it produces about 1.5 million tonnes.

s: Bhutan measures its success not by GDP but by Gross National Happiness — it is the only country in the world that has a formal GNH index as national policy.
q: Which country uniquely measures national success using a Gross National Happiness index?
e: Introduced in 1972 by Bhutan's fourth king, GNH measures psychological well-being, health, education, time use, cultural resilience, governance, ecology, community vitality, and living standards. It guides all government policy decisions.

s: The Salar de Uyuni in Bolivia is the world's largest salt flat — so perfectly flat that when covered by a thin layer of rain, it becomes the world's biggest natural mirror.
q: Where is the world's largest salt flat located?
e: Spanning 10,582 square kilometers at 3,656 meters elevation, this prehistoric lake bed contains about 10 billion tonnes of salt. It also holds about 50-70% of the world's known lithium reserves.

s: Bolivia has two capital cities — Sucre is the constitutional capital, while La Paz is the seat of government. La Paz, at 3,640 m elevation, is the world's highest administrative capital.
q: Which country has two capitals, with its administrative capital being the highest in the world?
e: After a civil war in 1899, Bolivia's government was moved to La Paz while Sucre retained its constitutional capital status. La Paz sits in a dramatic canyon at such high altitude that visitors often experience altitude sickness upon arrival.

s: Borneo, the world's third-largest island, is shared by three countries: Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei.
q: How many countries share the island of Borneo?
e: Indonesian Kalimantan covers about 73% of the island. Borneo is home to one of the oldest rainforests in the world, estimated at 130 million years old.

s: The Bosporus strait in Istanbul, Turkey, separates Europe from Asia and is only about 700 meters wide at its narrowest point.
q: Which strait separates Europe from Asia in Istanbul?
e: Istanbul is the only major city in the world that straddles two continents. About 48,000 ships pass through the Bosporus annually.

s: The Okavango Delta in Botswana is the world's largest inland delta — a river that never reaches the sea but instead fans out into the Kalahari Desert, creating an oasis.
q: What is unique about the Okavango Delta?
a: It is a river that never reaches the sea
d: It is the world's largest freshwater lake
It empties into the Atlantic Ocean
It has no visible water year-round
It is shared by five countries
It forms the border with three nations
e: The Okavango River flows from Angola into Botswana's Kalahari, where it spreads across 22,000 square kilometers of sand. This creates one of Africa's richest ecosystems in the middle of a desert.

s: The US-Canada border runs through a lake in Vermont-Quebec that has a library and opera house built directly on the international boundary — the Haskell Free Library has its entrance in the US and most of its books in Canada.
q: Which unusual library has its entrance in the US and most of its books in Canada?
e: Built in 1904 by the Haskell family, the library and attached opera house in Derby Line, Vermont / Stanstead, Quebec straddle the border. A black line on the floor marks the international boundary. Patrons enter from the US side but most shelves are in Canada.

s: Brazil is so large it spans three time zones, and during daylight saving time some regions used to have a fourth, meaning parts of the country could be four hours apart.
q: How many time zones does Brazil span?
e: Brazil covers nearly half of South America. Its westernmost state of Acre is two hours behind the capital Brasilia, and the Atlantic islands like Fernando de Noronha are one hour ahead.

s: The capital of Brunei is Bandar Seri Begawan.
q: What is the capital of Brunei?
e: Bandar Seri Begawan is the tropical capital of the island nation of Brunei!

s: Bulgaria produces about 85% of the world's rose oil, distilled from millions of Rosa damascena blossoms picked at dawn in the Valley of Roses each May and June.
q: Which country produces about 85% of the world's rose oil?
e: The Valley of Roses near Kazanlak has ideal conditions for rose cultivation. It takes roughly 3,000 kilograms of rose petals — about 1.5 million blossoms — to produce just one kilogram of rose oil, making it more expensive than gold by weight.

s: Burkina Faso hosts FESPACO, Africa's largest and oldest film festival, held biennially in Ouagadougou since 1969 and considered the 'Cannes of Africa.'
q: Which African country hosts the continent's largest film festival?
e: Despite being one of the world's poorest countries, Burkina Faso has been the cultural capital of African cinema for over 50 years. FESPACO attracts filmmakers from across the continent and has launched many of Africa's most celebrated directors.

s: Burundi's sacred royal drums, called karyenda, were so important that the drum was literally the symbol of the monarchy — the king's power was said to reside in the drum itself.
q: In which African country were royal drums the actual symbol of monarchical power?
e: The Ritual Dance of the Royal Drum is a UNESCO Intangible Heritage tradition. Up to a dozen drummers perform choreographed routines with massive drums carved from single tree trunks, representing courage, unity, and continuity of the nation.

s: In 1986, Lake Nyos in Cameroon suddenly released a massive cloud of CO2 gas that silently suffocated 1,746 people and 3,500 livestock in nearby villages overnight.
q: What deadly event occurred at Lake Nyos, Cameroon, in 1986?
a: A massive CO2 gas release suffocated nearby villages
e: Lake Nyos sits in a volcanic crater that leaks CO2 into the deep water. The gas built up over decades until the lake 'turned over,' releasing 1.6 million tonnes of CO2 at once. Engineers later installed degassing pipes to prevent a recurrence.

s: The capital of Canada is Ottawa.
q: What is the capital of Canada?
e: Ottawa serves as the administrative capital of Canada, located in the North America region. With a population of approximately 41.3 million, Canada is classified as a high income country by the World Bank.

s: Canada contains over 60% of the world's lakes — there are more lakes in Canada than in all other countries combined.
q: Which country contains more lakes than all other countries combined?
e: Canada has an estimated 879,800 lakes larger than 10 hectares. This incredible lake density is a legacy of glacial retreat after the last Ice Age, which carved basins and left meltwater across the Canadian Shield.

s: Canada has the longest coastline in the world at about 202,080 km, touching three oceans.
q: Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
e: Canada's coastline borders the Atlantic, Pacific, and Arctic oceans. If you walked 20 km per day, it would take you about 27 years to walk the entire coast.

q: What is the capital of Afghanistan?
e: Kabul is the capital city of Afghanistan.
